record.field: Zipline is a self-hosted ShareX and file upload server that turns your own infrastructure into a fast, feature-rich hub for screenshots, files and short links.

Zipline is a self-hosted ShareX and file upload server that turns your own infrastructure into a fast, feature-rich hub for screenshots, files and short links.

Built as a modern full-stack TypeScript application (Next.js frontend with a Node.js backend on PostgreSQL), Zipline pairs a polished dashboard with deep integrations for screenshot tools across Windows (ShareX), macOS (ishare), Linux (Flameshot) and mobile. It targets developers, teams and homelabbers who want full control over where their uploads live, with support for both local and S3-compatible storage. Its standout trait is being one of the most complete options in its class, combining uploads, URL shortening and rich embeds in a single app.

Key features
  • File uploads with folders, tags, chunked large-file transfers and on-the-fly image compression
  • URL shortener with custom links plus customizable embeds for Discord and other apps
  • Authentication via 2FA, passkeys and OAuth2/OIDC (Discord, GitHub, Google or any OIDC provider)
  • Password protection for files and URLs, user quotas and invite links
  • Fully featured API, Discord and HTTP webhooks, and installable PWA
  • Local and S3-compatible storage backends with Docker-first deployment
Best for

Developers, content creators and teams who want a self-hosted, privacy-respecting destination for screenshots and file sharing, and homelab users replacing third-party upload services with a single feature-complete server.

services: postgres: image: postgres:15 restart: unless-stopped environment: - POSTGRES_USER=postgres - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres - POSTGRES_DATABASE=postgres volumes: - ./pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -U postgres interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgres' networks: - internal zipline: image: ghcr.io/diced/zipline:v4 restart: unless-stopped environment: - CORE_RETURN_HTTPS={{ zipline_return_https }} - CORE_SECRET={{ zipline_secret }} - CORE_HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0 - CORE_PORT={{ zipline_port }} - DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@postgres/postgres - CORE_LOGGER={{ zipline_logger }} - DATASOURCE_TYPE=local - DATASOURCE_LOCAL_DIRECTORY=./uploads volumes: - ../files/uploads:/zipline/uploads - ../files/public:/zipline/public depends_on: - postgres container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:3000' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
